title: Hofbräuhaus am Platzl
raw_text:
{{Use dmy dates|date=March 2021}}
{{short description|Beer hall in the city center of Munich, Bavaria, Germany}}
{{coord|48|08|15|N|11|34|47|E|region:DE-BY_type:landmark|display=title}}
{{more citations needed|date=March 2021}}
[[File:Hofbrauhaus.JPG|thumb|Hofbräuhaus am Platzl, Munich]]
[[File:HB-Gastraum.1.JPG|thumb|Staff wear ''[[Tracht]]'' with men in ''[[Lederhosen]]'' and women in ''[[Dirndl]]'']]
[[File:2365 - München - Hofbräuhaus.JPG|thumb|The ceiling features elaborate frescoes in the baroque style.]]
[[Image:Hofbrauhaus beer.jpg|thumb|Beer is served in standard 1 liter mugs called ''[[Maß]]'', this one shown in the ''[[beer garden|Wirtsgarten]]''.]]

The '''Hofbräuhaus am Platzl''' is a [[beer hall]] in [[Munich]], [[Bavaria]], Germany, originally built in 1589 by [[List of rulers of Bavaria#Dukes of Bavaria 2|Bavarian Duke]] [[Duke Maximilian I of Bavaria|Maximilian I]] as an extension of the [[Staatliches Hofbräuhaus in München]] brewery. The general public was admitted in 1828 by [[Ludwig I of Bavaria|Ludwig I]]. The building was completely remodeled in 1897 by [[Max Littmann]] when the brewery moved to the suburbs. All of the rooms except the historic beer hall ("Schwemme") were destroyed in the [[World War II]] bombings. The reopening of the Festival Hall in 1958 marked the end of the post-war restoration work.

==History==
[[William V, Duke of Bavaria]] found the beer in Munich bad so he imported beer from Saxony.{{citation needed|date=November 2018}} He eventually asked his royal court to find a solution. A local brewery followed in 1589.{{citation needed|date=November 2018}} 

On February 24, 1920, the Hofbräuhaus am Platzl is where [[Nazi Germany]] [[dictator]] [[Adolf Hitler]] made a speech founding the [[National Socialist German Workers' Party]], or the Nazi Party.<ref name=gaab63>{{Cite book| last = Gaab| first = Jeffery S. | title = Munich: Hofbräuhaus & History : Beer, Culture, & Politics | publisher = Peter Lang Inc., International Academic Publishers | year = 2006| page = 63| isbn = 978-0820486062}}</ref>

The Hofbräuhaus was largely destroyed from allied bombing raids during World War II,<ref name=gaab83>{{Cite book| last = Gaab| first = Jeffery S. | title = Munich: Hofbräuhaus & History : Beer, Culture, & Politics | publisher = Peter Lang Inc., International Academic Publishers | year = 2006| page = 83| isbn = 978-0820486062}}</ref> but by Munich's 800th anniversary in 1958 the building had been faithfully reconstructed.<ref name=gaab96>{{Cite book| last = Gaab| first = Jeffery S. | title = Munich: Hofbräuhaus & History : Beer, Culture, & Politics | publisher = Peter Lang Inc., International Academic Publishers | year = 2006| page = 96| isbn = 978-0820486062}}</ref>

==Features==
[[File:Ottmar Zieher, München Hofbräuhaus Saal.jpg|thumb|right|The ''Hofbräuhaus Saal'' {{Circa|1902}}]]
The restaurant comprises most of the Hofbräuhaus am Platzl, which also includes a ballroom and outdoor ''[[beer garden|Wirtsgarten]]''. Its menu features such traditional favorites of [[Bavarian cuisine]] as ''Brezn'' ([[pretzel|soft pretzel]]), ''[[Obatzda]]'' (cheese dip), ''[[Hax'n]]'', and sausages such as ''[[Bratwurst]]'' and ''[[Weisswurst]]''. Brews include ''[[Helles]]'' and ''[[Dunkel|Dunkles]]'' served in a ''[[Maß]]'', ''[[wheat beer|Weißbier]]'', and [[wine]].

Munich's largest tourist attraction after the Oktoberfest, the Hofbräuhaus am Platzl is also frequented by locals, many of whom keep their personal mugs ("Krüge") stored there.<ref>This was introduced in 1972, when many of the clients did not want to exchange their "Keferloher" grey ceramic mugs for ordinary glass ones. There are 616 Krug-safes. https://www.sueddeutsche.de/muenchen/muenchen-hofbraeuhaus-masskrug-tresor-tradition-stammgast-stammtisch-1.4607381</ref> During regular hours traditional Bavarian music is played. The famous Hofbräuhaus song (Hofbräuhaus-Lied), composed in 1935 by Wilhelm "Wiga" Gabriel, goes: "''[[In München steht ein Hofbräuhaus]], oans, zwoa,  g'suffa!''{{-"}} ("There's a Hofbräuhaus in Munich—one, two, down the hatch!" in the local dialect).

== Innkeepers ==
* 1885–1896: Martin Ammerloher
* 1897–1906: Joseph Wittmann
* 1906–1919: Karl Mittermüller
* 1919–1930: Hans Parzer (during [[Hyperinflation in the Weimar Republic]])
* 1930–1945: Hans Bacherl (during Nazi rule in Germany and WWII)
* 1945–1950: Valentin Emmert (during [[Post-war#Year spans of the post-World War II era|Post-war]])
* 1950–1960: Franz Trimborn (during completion of reconstruction)
* 1960–1970: Toni Steiner
* 1970–1980: Hans Glanegger<ref>[http://www.hofbraeuhaus.de/de/01/hist_wirte.html www.hofbraeuhaus.de] {{in lang|de}} Die Wirte des Hofbräuhauses. Retrieved 29 July 2013</ref> 
* 1980–2004: Michael and Gerda Sperger<ref>Paul Brandt: ''Das Münchner Hofbräuhaus''. Dachau: Bayerland, 1997, {{ISBN|3-89251-232-9}}</ref>
* 2004–present: Wolfgang and Michael Sperger<ref>{{cite web
| url=http://www.hofbraeuhaus.de/de/01/hist_2004.html
| title=Wirtsfamilie Sperger in der zweiten Generation
| publisher=www.hofbraeuhaus.de
| accessdate=22 May 2011
| archive-date=27 June 2017
| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170627035725/http://www.hofbraeuhaus.de/de/01/hist_2004.html
| url-status=dead
}}</ref>

==Famous patrons==
[[Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart]] lived around the block from the beer hall in the late eighteenth century. In a poem he wrote, Mozart claimed to have written the opera ''[[Idomeneo]]'' after several visits to the Hofbräuhaus fortified him for the task.{{citation needed|date=November 2018}} In the nineteenth century, most of the breweries in Munich, including the Hofbräuhaus, were converted into large beer halls, restaurants, and entertainment centers with large, cavernous meeting rooms for weddings, concerts, and plays. In the period just before World War One, [[Vladimir Lenin]] lived in Munich and reportedly visited the Hofbräuhaus on a regular basis.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.rbth.com/history/335973-lenin-loved-beer | title=Lenin loved beer! But he was never seen drunk | work=Russia Beyond | date=18 March 2023 | last1=Manaev | first1=Georgy }}</ref> In 1919, the Munich Communist government set up headquarters in the beer hall, and in February 1920 [[Adolf Hitler]] and the [[Nazi Party|National Socialists]] held their first meeting in the Festsaal, the Festival Room, on the third floor.{{citation needed|date=November 2018}} 

On 24th February 1920, Hitler presented the Nazi Party's Twenty Five Point Program in the ''Hofbräuhaus''.<ref name="gaab63" /> On that day, the Nazi party held a large public meeting and whilst Hitler was speaking, the meeting erupted into a melee. There was a massive fight between the Social Democrat and Communist paramilitary opposition of the Nazi party, and the anti-fascists eventually lost the melee. Hitler managed to finish his address after the chaos of smashed tables and chairs and hurled beer mugs all about him. On 4 November 1921 the ''Hofbräuhaus'' was also the birthplace of the later feared Nazi paramilitary organization, the ''[[Sturmabteilung|Sturmabteilungen]]'', or SA. 

{{POV|date=July 2023}}
After Munich's world-famous [[Oktoberfest]] (where the Hofbräu has one of the largest beer tents), the Hofbräuhaus am Platzl is Munich's most outstanding tourist attraction and historical monument. Other famous visitors include [[Marcel Duchamp]], [[Thomas Wolfe]],<ref>Jeffrey Gaab, ''Munich: Hofbräuhaus and History'' (Peter Lang Publishers, NY, 2006)</ref> [[Louis Armstrong]], [[Mikhail Gorbachev]], NASA astronauts, and past American Presidents [[John F. Kennedy]] and [[George H. W. Bush]].{{citation needed|date=November 2018}}

<gallery widths="200" heights="200">
File:Adolf Hitler - Hofbräuhaus.jpg|Hofbräuhaus watercolor [[Paintings by Adolf Hitler|painting by Adolf Hitler]]
File:Mugs stored at the Hofbräuhaus am Platzl in Munich.jpg|Regular patrons store their mugs in small lockers
</gallery>

The '''WHU – Otto Beisheim School of Management''' is a [[:Category:Business schools in Germany|private German business school]] with campuses in [[Vallendar]] and [[Düsseldorf]], [[Germany]]. As of September 2024, there are 2,007 students (including doctoral students) at WHU, about 251 employees and 59 professors (including assistant professors).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.whu.edu/en/about-whu/|title=About WHU - whu.edu|website=www.whu.edu}}</ref>[[File:Vallendar Marienburg 86.JPG|thumb|The Marienburg in Vallendar is the main building of WHU.]]
== Academics ==

=== Rankings ===

==History==
WHU was founded in 1984 by the [[chamber of commerce|Koblenz Chamber of Commerce]] as the ''Wissenschaftliche Hochschule für Unternehmensführung'',<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Kaplan A.: European Management and European Business Schools: Insights from the History of Business Schools | doi=10.1016/j.emj.2014.03.006 | volume=32 | journal=European Management Journal | pages=529–534}}</ref> with preparations for the founding starting in 1983.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Die Erfolgsgeschichte der WHU |url=https://www.whu.edu/de/ueber-die-whu/geschichte-der-whu/ |access-date=2024-05-20 |website=WHU}}</ref> Starting in 1986, the school began offering a doctoral studies program, and the process for offering postdoctoral studies began in 1997.<ref name=":1" />

The name was modified in 1993 to honour WHU's benefactor, the businessman [[Otto Beisheim]], after he donated 50 Million [[Deutsche Mark]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Kaplan A.: European Management and European Business Schools: Insights from the History of Business Schools | doi=10.1016/j.emj.2014.03.006 | volume=32 | journal=European Management Journal | pages=529–534}}</ref><ref name=":1" />

In 1997, an executive MBA with [[Kellogg School of Management]] was started.<ref name=":1" /> A year later, the school received a [[EQUIS]]-accreditation, followed by a [[FIBAA]] in 2005 and the beginning of MBA studies the same year.<ref name=":1" /> In 2006, the school became a member of [[AACSB]], and was re-accredited by [[EQUIS]] in 2009.<ref name=":1" />

== Controversies ==

=== Accusations of problems with the internal culture and excessive stress since 2010 ===

The business school has been accused of fostering an overly competitive environment, even at the cost of the mental health of its students. Additionally, they were accused of overly focusing on rankings and grades but insufficiently developing the ability for critical discourse and not succeeding at creating a student body with an appropriate diversity of backgrounds.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |date=2013-05-01 |title=Kritik an Privatunis: Viel lernen, wenig diskutieren und hinterfragen |url=https://www.spiegel.de/lebenundlernen/uni/kritik-an-privatunis-viel-lernen-wenig-diskutieren-und-hinterfragen-a-884480.html |access-date=2024-02-08 |work=Der Spiegel |language=de |issn=2195-1349}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=gruenderszene |date=2020-07-21 |title=Mythos WHU: So tickt die junge Generation an der Gründer-Uni |url=https://www.businessinsider.de/gruenderszene/karriere-startup/mythos-whu-reportage/ |access-date=2024-02-08 |website=Business Insider |language=de-DE}}</ref> Additional criticisms, including by former students, often focus on excessive memorisation and a failure to foster a culture of out-of-the-box thinking.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-05-10 |title=SAAL ZWEI: "Business Schools züchten Nachwuchs, den die Gesellschaft nicht braucht" |url=http://www.saalzwei.de/exklusives/artikel/business-schools-zuechten-nachwuchs-den-die-gesellschaft-nicht-braucht/ |access-date=2024-02-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160510115158/http://www.saalzwei.de/exklusives/artikel/business-schools-zuechten-nachwuchs-den-die-gesellschaft-nicht-braucht/ |archive-date=2016-05-10 }}</ref><ref name=":0" />

After the death of a student who was believed to have died due to overworking himself during an internship but was found to have died due to an underlying condition, the school responded that high pressure is an unfortunately norm in the business world and that they are taking steps to address it.<ref>{{Cite web |last=gruenderszene |date=2020-07-21 |title=Mythos WHU: So tickt die junge Generation an der Gründer-Uni |url=https://www.businessinsider.de/gruenderszene/karriere-startup/mythos-whu-reportage/ |access-date=2024-02-08 |website=Business Insider |language=de-DE}}</ref>

=== Controversy about the academic work of [[Ulrich Lichtenthaler]] in 2012===

A former student and then-professor at the University, [[Ulrich Lichtenthaler]], was accused of repeated and significant misconduct in his research and publications.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2012-09-22 |title=Top-flight German business prof faces severe accusations of academic misconduct {{!}} Economics Intelligence |url=http://economicsintelligence.com/2012/07/19/top-flight-german-business-prof-faces-severe-accusations-of-academic-misconduct |access-date=2024-02-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120922093832/http://economicsintelligence.com/2012/07/19/top-flight-german-business-prof-faces-severe-accusations-of-academic-misconduct |archive-date=2012-09-22 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-08-14 |title=ulrich lichtenthaler |url=https://retractionwatch.com/category/by-author/ulrich-lichtenthaler/ |access-date=2024-02-21 |website=Retraction Watch |language=en-US}}</ref> 

WHU and the [[University of Mannheim]] later withdrew his right to teach, and he resigned from his position.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2013-09-21 |title=Vorwürfe unredlicher wissenschaftlicher Praxis gegen Professor Dr. Ulrich Lichtenthaler: Senat der WHU beschließt Aberkennung der Lehrbefähigung - whu.edu |url=http://www.whu.edu/ueber-die-whu/presse/pressemitteilungen/aktuelles-einzelansicht/article/vorwuerfe-unredlicher-wissenschaftlicher-praxis-gegen-professor-dr-ulrich-lichtenthaler-senat-der/ |access-date=2024-02-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130921035135/http://www.whu.edu/ueber-die-whu/presse/pressemitteilungen/aktuelles-einzelansicht/article/vorwuerfe-unredlicher-wissenschaftlicher-praxis-gegen-professor-dr-ulrich-lichtenthaler-senat-der/ |archive-date=2013-09-21 }}</ref>

== Notable alumni ==
* Verena Hubertz, German [[Federal Ministry for Housing, Urban Development and Building|Federal Minister for Housing, Urban Development and Building]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Verena Hubertz |url=https://www.bundesregierung.de/breg-en/federal-cabinet/2343396-2343396 |access-date=2025-05-08 |website=Website of the Federal Government {{!}} Bundesregierung |language=en}}</ref>
* [[Thomas Buberl]], CEO of [[Axa|Axa Group]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.axa.com/en/about-us/profile/thomas-buberl|title=Thomas Buberl | AXA|website=AXA.com}}</ref>
* Julian Deutz, CFO of [[Axel Springer SE|Axel Springer]], diploma<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.axelspringer.de/en/artikel/Curriculum-Vitae-Dr.-Julian-Deutz_19740891.html |title=Curriculum Vitae Dr. Julian Deutz Axel Springer SE |website=www.axelspringer.de |access-date=14 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140415050345/http://www.axelspringer.de/en/artikel/Curriculum-Vitae-Dr.-Julian-Deutz_19740891.html |archive-date=15 April 2014 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
* Robert Gentz, founder and board member of [[Zalando SE|Zalando]], diploma 2007<ref name="auto">{{cite web|url=https://corporate.zalando.com/en/company/management-board|title=The Management Board|website=Zalando Corporate Website}}</ref>
* Andreas Nick, Member of the Bundestag (German parliament), diploma 1990, PhD 1994<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bundestag.de/abgeordnete/biografien18/N/nick_andreas-258814|title=Deutscher Bundestag - Nick, Andreas|website=Deutscher Bundestag}}</ref>
* Matthias J. Rapp, CFO of TÜV SÜD AG<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.tuev-sued.de/company/tuev-sued-group/company-management-structure/tuev-sued-ag-board-of-management |title=Leadership | TÜV SÜD | TÜV SÜD |publisher=Tuev-sued.de |access-date=2020-02-13}}</ref>
* Oliver Samwer, CEO of [[Rocket Internet]]<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rocket-internet.com/about |title=About | Rocket Internet |access-date=2015-04-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150323143552/https://www.rocket-internet.com/about |archive-date=2015-03-23 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
* Christoph Schweizer, CEO of the [[Boston Consulting Group]]<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.bcg.com/en-gb/about/people/experts/christoph-schweizer | title=BCG's New CEO-Elect Christoph Schweizer | BCG }}</ref>
* David Schneider, founder and board member of Zalando, diploma 2007<ref name="auto"/>
* [[Margret Suckale]], board member of [[BASF]], EMBA<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.basf.com/de/company/about-us/management/board-of-executive-directors/margret-suckale.html |title=Margret Suckale |website=www.basf.com |access-date=14 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170303070639/https://www.basf.com/de/company/about-us/management/board-of-executive-directors/margret-suckale.html |archive-date=3 March 2017 |url-status=dead}}</ref>

clean_text: {{Infobox university | name = WHU – Otto Beisheim School of Management | image_name = WHU Logo.svg | motto = Excellence in management education | established = 1984 | type = Private research university Business school | rector = Professor Christian Andres | city = Vallendar | state = Rhineland-Palatinate | country = Germany | campus = Vallendar and Düsseldorf | students = 2,007 (September 2024) | faculty = 44 full professors, 15 assistant professors, 3 adjunct professors, 251 professional staff members (Sept. 2024) | colors = Blue and white | affiliations = AACSB, EQUIS, FIBAA | website = [http://www.whu.edu/ www.whu.edu] }} The WHU – Otto Beisheim School of Management is a private German business school with campuses in Vallendar and Düsseldorf, Germany. As of September 2024, there are 2,007 students (including doctoral students) at WHU, about 251 employees and 59 professors (including assistant professors).thumb|The Marienburg in Vallendar is the main building of WHU. WHU is regularly placed in the top positions in German university rankings. {| class="wikitable sortable" !Publisher !Ranking !Year !Worldwide !Germany |- | rowspan="6" |Financial Times |Global MBA |2024 |63rd |1st |- |Master in Management |2024 |14th |1st |- |Master in Finance |2024 |20th |1st |- |Top MBAs for Entrepreneurship | |6th |1st |- |Executive MBA |2024 |29th |2nd |- |European Business Schools | |16th |1st |- | rowspan="7" |QS |Top MBA |2025 |55th |3rd |- |Executive MBA (Joint Programs) |2024 |10th |. |- |Master in Management |2025 |24th |1st |- |Master in Finance |2025 |28th |1st |- |Subject Ranking: Business & Management Studies |2023 |128th |3rd |- |Subject Ranking: Accounting & Finance |2023 |101st-150th |2nd-4th |- |Subject Ranking: Business & Management Studies |2023 |451st-500th |17th |- | rowspan="3" |ARWU |Subject Ranking: Business Administration |2023 |101st-150th |1st |- |Subject Ranking: Finance |2023 |151st-200th |3rd-4th |- |Subject Ranking: Management |2023 |151st-200th |2nd |- |THE |Subject Ranking: Business and Economics |2018 |88th |5th |} WHU was founded in 1984 by the Koblenz Chamber of Commerce as the Wissenschaftliche Hochschule für Unternehmensführung, with preparations for the founding starting in 1983. Starting in 1986, the school began offering a doctoral studies program, and the process for offering postdoctoral studies began in 1997. Additional criticisms, including by former students, often focus on excessive memorisation and a failure to foster a culture of out-of-the-box thinking. A former student and then-professor at the University, Ulrich Lichtenthaler, was accused of repeated and significant misconduct in his research and publications.
