{{Short description|Christian missionary (born 1954)}} {{About|the street preacher|the Christian evangelist and comedian|Mike Warnke}} {{Multiple issues| {{Cleanup|reason=lots of verb-tense issues, out-of-date time periods, etc|date=January 2026}} {{POV|date=January 2026}} {{BLP sources|date=January 2026}} {{Primary sources|date=January 2026}} }} {{Use mdy dates|date=January 2019}} {{Infobox clergy | name = Michael Woroniecki | image =Street Preacher DNC Aug 28 2008.jpg | caption = Woroniecki at the 2008 [[Democratic National Convention]]. | birth_date ={{Birth date and age|1954|02|04}} | birth_place = [[Grand Rapids, Michigan]], US | death_date = | death_place = | church = Current: None Former: Cornerstone Christian Fellowship | other_names = Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War | education = [[Central Michigan University]]
[[Melodyland School of Theology]]
[[Fuller Theological Seminary]]
[[Aquinas College (Michigan)|Aquinas College]] }} '''Michael Peter Woroniecki''' (also '''Michael Warnecki''', '''Warneki''', '''Worneki''', '''Mike War''', and '''Mike Wazowski''';Suzy Spencer, Breaking Point, St. Martin's Library, p. 176,213 (Warnecki, Worneki "and a few other names") born February 4, 1954) is an American evangelical [[street preacher]] who has been described as a [[cult]] leader.{{cite AV media|people=Hakami, Elli (director), Hobbs, Julian P. (director)|date=2026|title=The Cult Behind the Killer: The Andrea Yates Story|type=television series|language=English|publisher=HBO Max|access-date=January 7, 2026|url=https://www.hbomax.com/shows/cult-behind-the-killer-the-andrea-yates-story/d6475c20-a627-4cdc-85a0-cb38d98aafd1}} Woroniecki is well known for his ministry on college campuses and at various public events across the US over the span of the last 30 years.{{cite magazine|url=http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1001706,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060923002641/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1001706,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 23, 2006|title=The Yates Odyssey|last=Berryman|first=Anne|author2=Fowler, Deborah |author3=Hylton, Hilary |author4= Fulton, Greg |date=July 26, 2006|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|accessdate=March 10, 2010}}{{Cite news|url=http://a.abcnews.com/Primetime/Story?id=2244865&page=1|title=Insanity Verdict|last=Cuomo|first=Chris|date=July 27, 2006|work=Primetime|publisher=ABC News|accessdate=March 10, 2010}}{{Cite news|url=https://abcnews.go.com/GMA/story?id=126222&page=1|title=I Shared Jesus With Them|date=March 27, 2002|work=Good Morning America|publisher=ABC News|accessdate=March 10, 2010|location=New York}} ==Early life and family== Woroniecki was the youngest of a large Polish [[Roman Catholic Church|Catholic]] family who was raised in the city of [[Grand Rapids, Michigan]]. His mother became involved in the [[Catholic Charismatic Movement]] in the early 1970s and sought to introduce her children to the [[Born again Christianity|born again]] experience. In 1972, seeking a way out of Grand Rapids, he "made a deal with God" that he would attend prayer meetings with his mother if he could make All-City [[Halfback (American football)|tailback]] in football and thus receive a scholarship for college. He got the title and the scholarship.{{Cite book|last=O'Malley|first=Suzanne|title="Are you there alone?" The unspeakable Crime of Andrea Yates|year=2004|publisher=Simon & Schuster|location=New York|pages=101|isbn=978-0-7432-4485-5}}{{Cite web|url=http://www.michaelpeterworoniecki.com/2010/01/michael-woroniecki-how-to-win-crazy-war.html|title=Michael Woroniecki "How To Win The Crazy War"|last=Woroniecki|first=Michael|accessdate=March 11, 2010}} Woroniecki attended [[Central Michigan University]] (CMU) where he studied psychology and played varsity football from 1972 to 1976. He boasted of being able to [[bench press]] 400 lbs and run a 4.5 second [[40-yard dash]]. Woroniecki explains in his Christian testimony that he forgot his deal with God and had a "wild streak", involving himself in alcohol and partying. Woroniecki states that he was known to his teammates as the "Crazy War" and says he was arrested the summer of his freshman year for fighting in a bar, just to prove to his peers how tough he was.{{Citation|last=Woroniecki|first=Michael|title=The Victory In Defeat|year=1994|url=http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/VictoryinDefeat.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304041118/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/VictoryinDefeat.pdf|archivedate=March 4, 2016}} During spring football practice in 1974, Woroniecki suffered a disabling football injury that threatened his athletic aspirations. About the same time, Woroniecki's mother gave him a [[Bible]], which he began to read. Woroniecki says God used this time of suffering and depression to break him of his pride, preparing him to receive the [[Gospel]].O'Malley, p. 102. Woroniecki attended the annual Catholic Charismatic Conference at [[University of Notre Dame]] the weekend of June 14, 1974 with his family. He was in the stadium when he says he told God that he didn't know what the saying "born again" meant, but that he wanted everything that the Lord had for him. While sitting alone in the stadium, Woroniecki says that he gave his life to Jesus. At that moment, Woroniecki believes that he "met the living Jesus". "The grass and goalposts were the same but I was changed" says Woroniecki, adding that he found significance in the recently painted ''[[Word of Life (mural)|Touchdown Jesus]]'' on the library mural right in front of him. Woroniecki returned to college football practice in July that same summer. One day after practice, while in a bar with his teammates, Woroniecki says he ordered water in the place of his usual beer. His friends becoming curious, Woroniecki explained that he had met Jesus. Used to his outrageous life-of-the-party humor, they all mistakenly thought he was joking, and Woroniecki became the focus of ridicule and rejection by his teammates. He writes that he could not understand why people like [[Roger Staubach]], a famous Christian athlete, were respected, yet he was rejected, until he read John 12:24 and 25. He reasoned that if the world hated Jesus without cause, they would also hate and reject him if he followed him. The same year, Woroniecki and his teammates went on to win the [[NCAA Division II Football Championship]] for the only time in Central Michigan's history.O'Malley, p. 103. Woroniecki graduated from Central Michigan with a [[Bachelor of Science|B.S.]] in [[Behavioral Sciences]] in 1976. While at CMU, Woroniecki met a [[cheerleader]] from [[Detroit|Detroit, Michigan]], Leslie Jean Ochalek (later renamed "Rachel Rebekah"). Woroniecki and Ochalek married in 1979. ==Education== Charles and Rose Woroniecki, Michael Woroniecki's parents, were members of the [[Basilica of St. Adalbert (Grand Rapids, Michigan)|Basilica of Saint Adalbert]], a Roman Catholic church in the Polish west side of Grand Rapids. Michael Woroniecki attended a Catholic grade school adjoining his family's [[parish]] and then advanced to [[West Catholic High School]], another [[parochial school]] in Grand Rapids. During his senior year of high school in 1972, Woroniecki began attending Catholic Charismatic prayer meetings, part of his "deal with God." After graduating from Central Michigan, Woroniecki attended [[Melodyland Christian Center#School of Theology|Melodyland School of Theology]] at [[Anaheim, California]] starting in 1976. A month after returning home from seminary that summer, his mother died from colon [[cancer]]. Woroniecki applied to the [[Dominican Order|Dominican]] and [[Franciscan]] Orders of the Catholic Church with the intention of helping to bring reform from within the Church. In his final round of interviews with the Dominican seminary near [[Chicago, Illinois]], Woroniecki was refused immediate admission and decided on a different course of action. He then applied to the Franciscan seminary in his own hometown of Grand Rapids. After a semester of study at [[Aquinas College (Michigan)|Aquinas College]], Woroniecki was again offered an extended preparatory period. He chose to pursue other means of study. In 1978, Woroniecki was accepted at [[Fuller Theological Seminary]] in [[Pasadena, California]], a non-denominational institution. Woroniecki says he was "deeply grieved" by the professionalism and business-like attitude towards the things of God at Fuller. This atmosphere, Woroniecki claims, ultimately compelled him to stand on the campus lawn and preach "the living Jesus" to fellow students and professors when they came out of the chapel. He confronted classmates over the attitudes of scholastic pride and hypocrisy that he thought to contradict the life of Jesus of Nazareth.O'Malley, p. 104. Woroniecki obtained his Master of Divinity degree from Fuller in 1980. Upon returning to Grand Rapids, Woroniecki briefly attended and occasionally preached at [[Maranatha Campus Ministries|Maranatha Church]]. Shortly thereafter, Woroniecki left to start his own home church and street preaching ministry.O'Malley, p. 105. ==Ministry== ===Preaching=== Woroniecki returned to Grand Rapids after graduating from Fuller Seminary in the summer of 1980, where he preached on the streets and at various events while carrying a sign or cross, starting his own ministry called Cornerstone Christian Fellowship. As a result of, according to Woroniecki, "youthful zeal" and a city unsure of how to handle his unconventional evangelistic approach, Woroniecki was arrested numerous times over the next year and a half.Evangelist Michael Woroniecki Meets the Press in the 'City of Churches, ''Curt Christy, December 1, 1981'' In October 1981, he offered to leave town if the pending charges against him would be dropped.Street Preacher Will Leave City to Beat Charges, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, October 8, 1981''Steve Grinczel,"Woman Prefers to See Street Preacher Tried", Grand Rapids Press, October 12, 1981Assistant City Attorney Michael D. McGuire, "Dissent!: Woroniecki Case, Grand Rapids Press, October 18, 1981 The offer was accepted and Woroniecki began to travel the US, stopping first in [[Atlanta, Georgia]] where street preaching is permitted.Follow Up: High Volume Street Preacher Reports Progress in Sunbelt, Grand Rapids Press, December 14, 1981"Traveling Preacher Arrested," Arizona State University ''State Press'', November 3, 1986.Jeanette Waite, "Anti-Mormon Protest Disturbs Campus", BYU Press, October 6, 1994 [http://newsnet.byu.edu/story.cfm/37644 Article] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040617152615/http://newsnet.byu.edu/story.cfm/37644 |date=June 17, 2004 }} To deliver his message, Woroniecki chooses college campuses, large sporting and political events as well as city centers. His wife and six adult children are often seen ministering alongside him.The Truth About Michael Woroniecki [http://www.michaelworoniecki.blogspot.com "Website"] {{Webarchive|url=https://archive.today/20121127070112/http://www.michaelworoniecki.blogspot.com/ |date=November 27, 2012 }} In the early 1990s, he shipped a Ford van to [[England]] and then ferried it to France. The family traveled together across Europe and then to Tangier, [[Morocco]]. In May 1995, they preached in [[Casablanca]], where Muslim law makes it a crime to declare Jesus as the Son of God. A riot resulted and the family was arrested. They were interrogated for eight hours by state officials, then ordered to leave the country.O'Malley, p. 106; Seattle Times News Services, Around the World--Rabat, Morocco, "Oregon Missionary Family Arrested in Casablanca", The Seattle Times, May 31, 1995; "Casablanca {{sic|?|Chrisitians|expected=Christians}}", USA Today (European Edition), approx. May 31 – June 2, 1995, p. 2. Since leaving Grand Rapids 30 years ago, Woroniecki and his family have preached in all 50 U.S. states, [[Latin America]], [[Europe]], [[Russia]] and [[Morocco]].[http://www.kaleo.org/home/index.cfm?event=displayArticle&ustory_id=dee202ef-d301-4a94-a4f9-cdb74541740a Article], Judgment Day? Freedom of Speech on Campus, John Nakatsu, Kaleo (The Voice), March 19, 2008Going for the Gold Steve; Grand Rapids Exile Preaches on at Olympics, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, August 4, 1996'' ===Music=== Michael Woroniecki began creating music with an electronic keyboard in 1986.Michael and David Woroniecki Bio on Reverb Nation.com [http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki#/page_object/page_object_bio/artist_656935 "Site"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019215059/http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki |date=October 19, 2012 }} In the early 1990s he began using this music as another form of ministry on the streets of [[Europe]]. During the summers of 1992 to 1996 he performed in downtown city plazas and centers in places like [[London]], [[Paris]], [[Barcelona]], [[Lisbon]], [[Rome]], [[Berlin]], [[Budapest]], [[Athens]], and [[Moscow]]. Woroniecki says he used the music along with dances and Biblical skits as a means of communicating the gospel in countries where they did not speak the language. Michael does not consider himself professional but believes "that music is a powerful tool to touch people of all walks of life". During the 1996 Atlanta [[Summer Olympic Games]], Woroniecki was reported to have performed with his family outside the various venues using the event to reach the international crowds with his message. Beginning in 1997, Woroniecki traveled throughout [[Mexico]] and [[Latin America]], playing on the streets. Woroniecki claims that, from 2006 to 2009, he has self-produced eight music CDs which he and his family have distributed in their travels.Michael and David Woroniecki on Reverb Nation [http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki "Website"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019215059/http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki |date=October 19, 2012 }}Music by Michael Woroniecki: Time Seizure-''2002-English'', Traveline: ¿Donde Puedes Ir?-''2003-Spanish / English'', Measures of a New World-''2008-English'', Las Alturas de los Quebrantados-''2009-Spanish / English '', Mi Amigo-''2009-Instrumental'', Aperture, 2009-InstrumentalVaried Followers share Faith with Campus, ''The BG News (Bowling Green), Stephanie Guigou, September 25, 2008'' [http://issuu.com/bgviews/docs/bgnews092508 "Archive"] ==Religious views== [[File:WoronieckiwithsigninTimesSquare.jpg|thumb|left|x200px|alt=Woroniecki carries sign in Times Square that reads "Discover forgiveness for all your sin and eternal life in the Living Jesus".|Woroniecki in Times Square, 2008.]] Woroniecki preaches that one must "get alone, study the [[New Testament]] and seek the Living [[Jesus]] to be Born Again".God's Warriors come to UCO, ''Aaron Wright, The Vista, University of Central OK., August 28, 2007'' [http://www.thevistaonline.com/2.8883/god-s-warriors-come-to-uco-1.1154838 "Archive"]{{dead link|date=December 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} He says that today's "churches" are not the true Church as seen in the Scriptures and cannot save anyone.Spencer, p. 137. Woroniecki has used large banner signs and often a wooden cross to convey his message for the past 35 years.Michael Woroniecki preaches Jesus not murder, ''Central Michigan Life, Heather Bellife, May 22, 2002'' [http://www.cm-life.com/2002/05/22/michaelworonieckipreachesjesusnotmurder "Archive"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100303012653/http://www.cm-life.com/2002/05/22/michaelworonieckipreachesjesusnotmurder/ |date=March 3, 2010 }} In his ministry Woroniecki addresses the Biblical themes of hypocrisy in the institutional system, [[Grace (Christianity)|grace]], [[salvation]], forgiveness, [[hell]], [[judgment]], deception and the consequences of sin. Because of this, Woroniecki has taken his share of verbal and physical opposition. He claims that these are the "last days" and that Jesus will soon return to judge the world.Interview with Woroniecki family member outside the Republican National Convention, Baron Dave Romm, KFAI-News FM 90.3 and 106.7, Minneapolis-St. Paul, MN, September 3, 2008 [http://www.romm.org/audio/20080903rnc28joshua.mp3]Explosion, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2003''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Xplosion.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012734/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Xplosion.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }} Woroniecki cites various Scripture verses in his pamphlets to substantiate his message that only by faith can a man be saved from hell and reconciled to God, a doctrine accepted by most mainstream Christians and defined by Martin Luther as [[sola fide]].Fact or Fiction, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2002''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Fact%20or%20Fiction.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012731/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Fact%20or%20Fiction.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }}What About YOU?, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2003''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/What%20About%20YOU.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012733/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/What%20About%20YOU.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }}Slammed, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2004'' [http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Slammed.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012732/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Slammed.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }} The Bible figures strongly in Woroniecki's beliefs and is a focal point of his preaching. Despite not holding a conventional position in an established religion, Woroniecki's knowledge of Scripture and theology is extensive.; William Holm, "A Prophet Without Honor", Grand Rapids Magazine, February 1981 His years of personal and scholastic study at three seminaries are reflected in his writings. Woroniecki believes in [[Biblical inerrancy]] and considers the Scriptures authoritative on all matters of life. Woroniecki's message is met on the streets with a variety of responses.Jerry Morlock, "Disturbing Word Jails Evangelist", Grand Rapids Press, September 14, 1980Spencer, pp 177,178.Steve Grinczel, "Woman Prefers to See Street Preacher Tried", Grand Rapids Press, October 12, 1981 ; Stuart Villanueva, "Religious Conflict", The Battalion, Texas A&M University, April 26, 2001 ==Andrea Yates== Woroniecki first met Rusty Yates in the mid-1980s while preaching on the campus of [[Auburn University]]. Rusty took some literature, and they corresponded by mail for the next several years. In 1989 Rusty introduced the Woronieckis to [[Andrea Yates|Andrea Kennedy]], whom he had recently begun dating.O'Malley, p. 28. Woroniecki said of this meeting that Andrea seemed slightly intimidated, and that he advised them not to marry quickly, the same advice he gave to other young couples.O'Malley, p. 29. The Yates and Woroniecki families stayed in touch by letter and telephone, and in 1998, the Yates family traveled to Miami after hearing that the Woronieckis were selling their motor home.O'Malley, p. 31. Rusty bought the bus, a 1978 GMC that Woroniecki had used for his family's travels. During the week that the Yates stayed, Woroniecki confronted Rusty, saying that he was "willing to sacrifice his wife and children for the sake of his job".{{Cite news|title=A preacher speaks out|date=March 20, 2002|work=Dateline NBC|publisher=NBC News}}{{cite news|title=Unrepentant in Yates tragedy|last=Nichols|first=Bruce|date=April 20, 2002|newspaper=The Toronto Star}} After this confrontation, Rusty and Woroniecki "grew apart". In the months leading up to the drownings, Rusty described the relationship with the Woronieckis as not being very close. Andrea Yates had suffered mental health difficulties for several years leading up to 2001. In June 1999 Andrea took an overdose of sedatives and was hospitalized several times over the next two years. She was prescribed a variety of antidepressant and antipsychotic medications. On June 20, 2001, a few weeks after being discharged from Devereaux Texas Treatment Center, Andrea killed all five of her children.O'Malley, chapters 2 & 3 In early 2002 Michael Woroniecki came to the attention of the national media when Yates's trial started. Yates's defense attorney, George Parnham, placed a copy of Woroniecki's newsletter ''The Perilous Times'' into evidence,{{Cite news|title=Examining a spiritual leader's influence|last=Gesalman|first=Anne Belli|date=March 18, 2002|work=Newsweek|pages=8}} suggesting that the material was dangerous for someone like Yates,O'Malley, p. 175. and defense team psychiatrist Lucy Puryear said on ''[[Good Morning America]]'' that "Yates's fate may have been different if she never met Woroniecki". Certain media outlets alleged that Woroniecki bore some responsibility for influencing Andrea.O'Malley, p. 109. However, both Michael Woroniecki and Rusty Yates dismissed the allegations. According to the ''[[Houston Chronicle]]'', "[Russell] Yates said his relationship with Woroniecki was more like learning from a book. He was free to accept what he wanted and reject that which he didn't".Duke Journal of Gender Law and Policy, ''Volume 10:1, 2003 Appendix 1'', Timeline of Andrea Yates Life and Trial [http://www.law.duke.edu/journals/djglp/downloads/gen10p61.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305205404/http://www.law.duke.edu/journals/djglp/downloads/gen10p61.pdf |date=March 5, 2012 }} Yates said "That's just crazy" when asked whether or not his wife was influenced by Woroniecki.Lost in the message? Cleric says he's not to blame for Yates demons, ''Lisa Teachy, The Houston Chronicle, April 6, 2002'' [http://www.chron.com/CDA/archives/archive.mpl?id=2002_3533886 "Archive"] He stated that Andrea had suffered schizophrenia, depression and delusions for years. Both Yates and Woroniecki characterized the connection between Woroniecki's teachings and Andrea Yates's state of mind as nothing more than [[sensationalism|sensationalist reporting]]. Woroniecki "... certainly didn't cause the delusion," says Yates. Woroniecki called the defense's claims "ridiculous". He stated that he had only met Andrea personally four or five times over 20 years and that his relationship with her was "one of nothing but love and compassion". Woroniecki said that he had warned Russell Yates that Andrea and their children needed his love.[They Needed Jesus, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, March 9, 2002''] [[Peter Jennings]] of ''[[World News with Diane Sawyer|World News Tonight]]'' reported that Rachel Woroniecki wrote to Rusty and Andrea that they needed to reconcile their marriage.Did the Preacher see it coming?, ''World News Tonight, Peter Jennings, January 21, 2002'' In an interview with ''[[Dateline NBC]]'' in March 2002, Mrs. Woroniecki said:
"A major problem is that people think that by emulating our lifestyle they can have the joy and the love that we have without building a foundation in Jesus Christ."
Woroniecki was quoted by the ''[[Grand Rapids Press]]'' as saying,
"I will gladly sacrifice my reputation if it can spare Andrea from the death penalty and give her a second chance at life. However ... it is deceitful and irresponsible to blame doctors, hospitals, clinics or 'postpartum depression.' They (Rusty and Andrea Yates) both know that the issues which culminated in this tragedy are much, much deeper."
==Currently== Woroniecki and family members carried signs and preached outside both the [[Democratic National Convention]] in [[Denver]] and the [[Republican National Convention]] in [[Saint Paul, Minnesota|Saint Paul]] in 2008.[http://www.mlive.com/news/grand-rapids/index.ssf/2008/09/street_preacher_family_undaunt.html Street preacher, family undaunted by jeers] The Grand Rapids Press, September 23, 2008 In November 2010, Woroniecki and his family were seen ministering in Times Square in New York City, promoting their website www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com. The author writes that the primary point of their message was "that Jesus is alive and that we need to actively seek him out", adding that despite any controversy, the family seemed to him to be genuine in their efforts to "go all over the world and celebrate life and the living Jesus."[http://voices.yahoo.com/woroniecki-family-times-square-7122832.html?cat=34 Woroniecki Family in Times Square] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140518061214/http://voices.yahoo.com/woroniecki-family-times-square-7122832.html?cat=34 |date=May 18, 2014 }} by Jesse Schmitt, November 8, 2010 Jack Wellman, Christian evangelist, author and regular contributor to ''OVI Magazine'' and ''[[Yahoo! Voices]]'', confirms that Woroniecki continues to take his message "to the world and the streets of the nations". Woroniecki's current message, according to Wellman, taken from Woroniecki's website and literature, is:
"Are you willing to look deeper, beyond it all? Please give us a chance to help you. It will be well worth your time. What we offer you is not a new group, "church", formulas, self exaltation or doctrines. What we offer you is the love of the living Jesus who has the answers that you are looking for".[http://www.ovimagazine.com/art/6491 Is Anyone Thirsty? The Woroniecki Family's Story] by Jack Wellman, Nov. 17, 2010[http://www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com If Anyone Is Thirsty ] Michael Woroniecki's Website
Woroniecki and his family currently run a 501(c)3 organization called Deep Heart, which is the organization they often tour under.{{Cite web |last=Roberts |first=Andrea Suozzo, Alec Glassford, Ash Ngu, Brandon |date=2013-05-09 |title=Deep Heart - Nonprofit Explorer |url=https://projects.propublica.org/nonprofits/organizations/844110211 |access-date=2026-02-05 |website=ProPublica |language=en}} Woroniecki remains active in his ministry and has recently visited various universities during the fall semesters.[http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2004/09/09-23-04tdc/09-23-04dnews-07.asp Traveling preachers descend onto PSU] {{webarchive|url=https://archive.today/20041009220954/http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2004/09/09-23-04tdc/09-23-04dnews-07.asp |date=October 9, 2004 }} September 23, 2004[http://www.thepenn.org/news/2004/09/27/News/Zealots.Preach.In.Oak.Grove-2229921.shtml Zealots preach in Oak Grove]{{dead link|date=December 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} September 27, 2004 The Penn, [[Indiana University of Pennsylvania]][https://web.archive.org/web/20041105154618/http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2004/09/09-30-04tdc/09-30-04dnews-17.asp Yates was one of Woroniecki's followers] September 30, 2004[http://www.collegiatetimes.com/news/1/ARTICLE/3980/2004-10-05.html Religious solicitors harass students] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060405133935/http://www.collegiatetimes.com/news/1/ARTICLE/3980/2004-10-05.html |date=April 5, 2006 }} October 5, 2004 The Collegiate Times, Virginia Polytechnic & State University[http://www.collegiatetimes.com/news/2/ARTICLE/3996/2004-10-06.html Religious enthusiasts identified] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927104314/http://www.collegiatetimes.com/news/2/ARTICLE/3996/2004-10-06.html |date=September 27, 2007 }} October 6, 2004 [https://web.archive.org/web/20060906113111/http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2005/10/10-18-05tdc/10-18-05dnews-03.asp Family warns, preaches: 'We are ... going to Hell'] October 18, 2005 The Digital Collegian, PSU [http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2006/09/09-22-06tdc/09-22-06dnews-09.asp Traveling preachers stir controversy] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070524060819/http://www.collegian.psu.edu/archive/2006/09/09-22-06tdc/09-22-06dnews-09.asp |date=May 24, 2007 }} Article from the Pennsylvania State Daily Collegian, September 22, 2005 ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *O'Malley, Suzanne. Are You There Alone?: The Unspeakable Crime of Andrea Yates, Simon & Schuster, 2004. {{ISBN|978-0743244855}}. *Spencer, Suzy. Breaking Point, St. Martin's Paperbacks, 2002. {{ISBN|978-0312983093}}. ==External links== *[http://www.michaelpeterworoniecki.com/ The Truth About Michael Woroniecki] - Joshua Woroniecki's blog *[http://www.michaelworoniecki.com/ michaelworoniecki.com] - The Official Website of the Woroniecki family {{1974 Central Michigan Chippewas football navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Woroniecki, Michael Peter}} [[Category:1954 births]] [[Category:Christian fundamentalism]] [[Category:Living people]] [[Category:Campus preachers]] [[Category:Central Michigan Chippewas football players]] [[Category:American football running backs]] [[Category:Aquinas College (Michigan) alumni]] [[Category:Former Roman Catholics]] [[Category:American Christian missionaries]] [[Category:American people of Polish descent]] [[Category:Fuller Theological Seminary alumni]] [[Category:People from Grand Rapids, Michigan]]
clean_text: {{Multiple issues| }} {{Infobox clergy | name = Michael Woroniecki | image =Street Preacher DNC Aug 28 2008.jpg | caption = Woroniecki at the 2008 Democratic National Convention. | birth_date = | birth_place = Grand Rapids, Michigan, US | death_date = | death_place = | church = Current: None Former: Cornerstone Christian Fellowship | other_names = Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War | education = Central Michigan UniversityMelodyland School of TheologyFuller Theological SeminaryAquinas College }} Michael Peter Woroniecki (also Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War, and Mike Wazowski; born February 4, 1954) is an American evangelical street preacher who has been described as a cult leader. Woroniecki is well known for his ministry on college campuses and at various public events across the US over the span of the last 30 years. Woroniecki was the youngest of a large Polish Catholic family who was raised in the city of Grand Rapids, Michigan. His mother became involved in the Catholic Charismatic Movement in the early 1970s and sought to introduce her children to the born again experience. In 1972, seeking a way out of Grand Rapids, he "made a deal with God" that he would attend prayer meetings with his mother if he could make All-City tailback in football and thus receive a scholarship for college. He got the title and the scholarship. Woroniecki attended Central Michigan University (CMU) where he studied psychology and played varsity football from 1972 to 1976. He boasted of being able to bench press 400 lbs and run a 4.5 second 40-yard dash. During spring football practice in 1974, Woroniecki suffered a disabling football injury that threatened his athletic aspirations. About the same time, Woroniecki's mother gave him a Bible, which he began to read. Woroniecki says God used this time of suffering and depression to break him of his pride, preparing him to receive the Gospel. Woroniecki attended the annual Catholic Charismatic Conference at University of Notre Dame the weekend of June 14, 1974 with his family. He was in the stadium when he says he told God that he didn't know what the saying "born again" meant, but that he wanted everything that the Lord had for him. While sitting alone in the stadium, Woroniecki says that he gave his life to Jesus. At that moment, Woroniecki believes that he "met the living Jesus". "The grass and goalposts were the same but I was changed" says Woroniecki, adding that he found significance in the recently painted Touchdown Jesus on the library mural right in front of him. Woroniecki graduated from Central Michigan with a B.S. in Behavioral Sciences in 1976. While at CMU, Woroniecki met a cheerleader from Detroit, Michigan, Leslie Jean Ochalek (later renamed "Rachel Rebekah"). Woroniecki and Ochalek married in 1979. Woroniecki obtained his Master of Divinity degree from Fuller in 1980. Woroniecki returned to Grand Rapids after graduating from Fuller Seminary in the summer of 1980, where he preached on the streets and at various events while carrying a sign or cross, starting his own ministry called Cornerstone Christian Fellowship. In October 1981, he offered to leave town if the pending charges against him would be dropped. The offer was accepted and Woroniecki began to travel the US, stopping first in Atlanta, Georgia where street preaching is permitted. To deliver his message, Woroniecki chooses college campuses, large sporting and political events as well as city centers. His wife and six adult children are often seen ministering alongside him. In the early 1990s, he shipped a Ford van to England and then ferried it to France. The family traveled together across Europe and then to Tangier, Morocco. In May 1995, they preached in Casablanca, where Muslim law makes it a crime to declare Jesus as the Son of God. A riot resulted and the family was arrested. They were interrogated for eight hours by state officials, then ordered to leave the country. Since leaving Grand Rapids 30 years ago, Woroniecki and his family have preached in all 50 U.S. states, Latin America, Europe, Russia and Morocco. Michael Woroniecki began creating music with an electronic keyboard in 1986. In the early 1990s he began using this music as another form of ministry on the streets of Europe. During the summers of 1992 to 1996 he performed in downtown city plazas and centers in places like London, Paris, Barcelona, Lisbon, Rome, Berlin, Budapest, Athens, and Moscow. thumb|left|x200px|alt=Woroniecki carries sign in Times Square that reads "Discover forgiveness for all your sin and eternal life in the Living Jesus".|Woroniecki in Times Square, 2008. Woroniecki preaches that one must "get alone, study the New Testament and seek the Living Jesus to be Born Again". He says that today's "churches" are not the true Church as seen in the Scriptures and cannot save anyone. Woroniecki has used large banner signs and often a wooden cross to convey his message for the past 35 years. Woroniecki cites various Scripture verses in his pamphlets to substantiate his message that only by faith can a man be saved from hell and reconciled to God, a doctrine accepted by most mainstream Christians and defined by Martin Luther as sola fide. The Bible figures strongly in Woroniecki's beliefs and is a focal point of his preaching. Despite not holding a conventional position in an established religion, Woroniecki's knowledge of Scripture and theology is extensive. His years of personal and scholastic study at three seminaries are reflected in his writings. Woroniecki first met Rusty Yates in the mid-1980s while preaching on the campus of Auburn University. Rusty took some literature, and they corresponded by mail for the next several years. In 1989 Rusty introduced the Woronieckis to Andrea Kennedy, whom he had recently begun dating. Woroniecki said of this meeting that Andrea seemed slightly intimidated, and that he advised them not to marry quickly, the same advice he gave to other young couples. The Yates and Woroniecki families stayed in touch by letter and telephone, and in 1998, the Yates family traveled to Miami after hearing that the Woronieckis were selling their motor home. Rusty bought the bus, a 1978 GMC that Woroniecki had used for his family's travels. After this confrontation, Rusty and Woroniecki "grew apart". In early 2002 Michael Woroniecki came to the attention of the national media suggesting that the material was dangerous for someone like Yates, and defense team psychiatrist Lucy Puryear said on Good Morning America that "Yates's fate may have been different if she never met Woroniecki". However, both Michael Woroniecki and Rusty Yates dismissed the allegations. According to the Houston Chronicle, "[Russell] Yates said his relationship with Woroniecki was more like learning from a book. He was free to accept what he wanted and reject that which he didn't". Yates said "That's just crazy" when asked whether or not his wife was influenced by Woroniecki. He stated that Andrea had suffered schizophrenia, depression and delusions for years. Peter Jennings of World News Tonight reported that Rachel Woroniecki wrote to Rusty and Andrea that they needed to reconcile their marriage. In an interview with Dateline NBC in March 2002, Mrs. Woroniecki said: "A major problem is that people think that by emulating our lifestyle they can have the joy and the love that we have without building a foundation in Jesus Christ." Woroniecki was quoted by the Grand Rapids Press as saying, "I will gladly sacrifice my reputation if it can spare Andrea from the death penalty and give her a second chance at life. However ... it is deceitful and irresponsible to blame doctors, hospitals, clinics or 'postpartum depression.' They (Rusty and Andrea Yates) both know that the issues which culminated in this tragedy are much, much deeper." In November 2010, Woroniecki and his family were seen ministering in Times Square in New York City, promoting their website www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com. The author writes that the primary point of their message was "that Jesus is alive and that we need to actively seek him out", adding that despite any controversy, the family seemed to him to be genuine in their efforts to "go all over the world and celebrate life and the living Jesus." Jack Wellman, Christian evangelist, author and regular contributor to OVI Magazine and Yahoo! Voices, confirms that Woroniecki continues to take his message "to the world and the streets of the nations". Woroniecki's current message, according to Wellman, taken from Woroniecki's website and literature, is: "Are you willing to look deeper, beyond it all? Please give us a chance to help you. It will be well worth your time. What we offer you is not a new group, "church", formulas, self exaltation or doctrines. What we offer you is the love of the living Jesus who has the answers that you are looking for". Woroniecki and his family currently run a 501(c)3 organization called Deep Heart, which is the organization they often tour under. Woroniecki remains active in his ministry and has recently visited various universities during the fall semesters. *O'Malley, Suzanne. Are You There Alone?: The Unspeakable Crime of Andrea Yates, Simon & Schuster, 2004. . *Spencer, Suzy. Breaking Point, St. Martin's Paperbacks, 2002. . *[http://www.michaelpeterworoniecki.com/ The Truth About Michael Woroniecki] - Joshua Woroniecki's blog *[http://www.michaelworoniecki.com/ michaelworoniecki.com] - The Official Website of the Woroniecki family Category:1954 births Category:Christian fundamentalism Category:Living people Category:Campus preachers Category:Central Michigan Chippewas football players Category:American football running backs Category:Aquinas College (Michigan) alumni Category:Former Roman Catholics Category:American Christian missionaries Category:American people of Polish descent Category:Fuller Theological Seminary alumni Category:People from Grand Rapids, Michigan

title: Christian Apologetics and Research Ministry
clean_text: {{Multiple issues| }} {{Infobox organization |name = Christian Apologetics and Research Ministry |logo = Christian Apologetics and Research Ministry logo.png |image_alt = |caption = |abbreviation = CARM |formation = 1995 |type = 501(c)(3) |status = Active |purpose = Protestant evangelism |location = United States |leader_title = Founder/President |leader_name = Matthew Slick |board_of_directors = Matthew Slick, David Kimball, Charlie Spine |website = }} The Christian Apologetics and Research Ministry (CARM) is a nonprofit, nondenominational Protestant apologetics ministry with an internet and radio outreach. It is involved in evangelism, including full-time support for several foreign missionaries. It is based in the United States and was founded in 1995. Matthew Slick currently serves as president of the ministry. The ministry is registered as a 501(c)(3) organization and is headquartered in Nampa, Idaho. In November 1995, Matthew Slick compiled his sermons and notes together onto computer, and created a website for the Christian Research Ministry. By 2000, Slick said that his website was receiving 14,000 visits per week. In 2004, CARM made available a free resource called the Dictionary of Theology for the Palm OS system. The website also provides Protestants with pre-formatted "cut-and-paste" arguments to use in online forums with atheists, relativists, Muslims, Mormons, Jehovah's Witnesses, Catholics and members of other groups. CARM is a Protestant ministry, involving several different branches of Protestantism. For example, CARM's official position is that the Calvinist interpretation of Christianity is "within orthodoxy," but does not brand itself a Calvinist ministry, saying that both Calvinists and non-Calvinists write articles. Conservative columnist Cal Thomas of Tribune Media Services comments, "Christian Apologetics and Research Ministry (the Web site carm.org) has created a useful chart that shows the conflicting claims of classic Christian belief and Muslim doctrines. It is worth studying, whatever one's faith." Christian Parenting Today notes that the website of CARM provides "lists, definitions, and descriptions of cults", to assist parents and children with identifying controversial groups and movements. The Ontario Consultants on Religious Tolerance says of CARM, "This is a very large web site. It is rated by Hitbox.com as the most visited counter-cult website, and about #14 in the list of most-visited religious web sites." The Gazette recommended CARM as a resource for information on apologetics. Writing in the South Florida Sun-Sentinel, Pastor Bob Coy of Calvary Chapel, Fort Lauderdale characterized CARM among "excellent resources… that will allow those who are seriously searching to discover faith is more fact than fiction." In the book The New Media Frontier: Blogging, Vlogging, and Podcasting for Christ, Roger Overton, a blogger and graduate student at Talbot School of Theology, recommends CARM as a resource, calling the organization's website "an informative site dealing with topics from the defense of mere Christianity to exposing the problems in cults and other religions. Go to the CARM website for the straight facts such as a list of the prophecies Jesus fulfilled or archived incriminating statements by the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (Mormons) and The Watchtower (Jehovah's Witnesses)." Thomas Nelson's Safe Sites Internet Yellow Pages, The 2000–2001 Edition describes the organization as "A Christian ministry promoting Christian truth with articles on doctrine, Mormons, Jehovah's Witnesses, Evolution, New Age, atheism, and more." The book recommends the organization's Theological Dictionary as among the "Best of the Christian Web", saying it "Defines many Christian and theological terms." * * * * * Christian countercult movement * List of Christian apologetic works * Category:Calvinist organizations established in the 20th century Category:Charities based in New York (state) Category:Christian charities based in the United States Category:Christian countercult organizations Category:Christian websites Category:Meridian, Idaho Category:Christian organizations established in 1995

'''Ben Wicks''', {{Post-nominals|country=CAN|CM}} (born '''Alfred Wicks'''; October 1, 1926 – September 10, 2000) was a British-born Canadian cartoonist, illustrator, journalist and author.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.thecanadianencyclopedia.ca/en/article/alfred-wicks/|title= Alfred Wicks|author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |website= The Canadian Encyclopedia|publisher= Historica Canada|access-date= 21 October 2016}}</ref>

==Biography==
Wicks was a [[Cockney]] born into a poor, [[working class|working-class]] family in London's [[East End of London|East End]] near [[London Bridge]]. He learned to play the [[saxophone]] in the [[British Army]] and toured Europe in a band with author Leonard Bigg also from London. He immigrated to Canada in 1957 with his wife [[Doreen Wicks]] with just $25. He found work as a milkman in [[Calgary]] and then joined the [[Canadian Army]] as a musician and began studying cartooning from books. Wicks came across a list in a library of magazines willing to purchase cartoons and began trying his hand—his first major success was being published by the ''[[Saturday Evening Post]]''.

In 1963, he travelled to Toronto to assess cartooning possibilities and met Toronto Telegram 'The Giants' daily illustrated feature cartoonist, Norman Drew, who advised him to move to Toronto. Wicks then moved to Toronto to work for the ''[[Toronto Telegram]]'' and his cartoon, ''The Outcasts'', was soon syndicated in over 50 newspapers. His cartoons were simply drawn but were very topical and witty and became popular with readers and were picked up by the ''[[Toronto Star]]'' after the ''Telegram'' ceased operations in 1971. At its height, his daily cartoon, now called ''Wicks'', was carried by 84 Canadian and more than 100 American newspapers.

Wicks had a self-effacing but charming personality and became a popular guest on television and radio shows and had his own television show on the [[Canadian Broadcasting Corporation]] in the 1970s. He also created and illustrated the ''[[Katie and Orbie]]'' series of children's books written by his daughter Susan, which in 1994 were turned into an animated series for [[Family Channel (Canadian TV network)|Family]] in Canada and for [[Public Broadcasting Service|PBS]] in the United States.

{{blockquote|I was the first person to employ Ben Wicks when he arrived in Calgary early in 1957. I was a partner in Fisher Trade Services Ltd., a company set up to supply the printing industry with camera-ready art and short run printing projects. An immigrant myself from Britain in 1952, I was trained at the old Luton School of Arts, now [[Barnfield College]], and had just left my job as head of a small art and printing department at Motor Car Supply Company to form a partnership with Art Brown, of Graphic Arts Supply Ltd.

Ben had been canvassing all the suitable companies in Calgary, and came into my place one day accompanied by his wife Doreen. I explained we had just started operations and simply couldn't afford to take on staff. Ben promptly offered to work for nothing on a trial period and I was impressed by his persistence and determination, plus his obvious ability to get along with people. He showed me some samples of his work and I was frankly unimpressed, but there was something about him that appealed to me. I was in need of a short holiday as the stress of our start-up was beginning to tell so I decided to take him at his word. Ben was something of a disaster during his stay with me as he simply hadn't the experience and speed needed to cope with the world of commercial art, but we parted friends after a short time and stayed friends for many years. Ben was godfather to our son Michael John Fisher, and was a frequent house-guest during his early years in Canada.

Ben was a professional charmer in the best sense of that word. He was a hard worker and endured all the rejections and disappointments that go with trying to break into the cartoon business with good humour and a ready smile. His cockney accent was his [[schtick]] and as the years went by it seemed to grow more pronounced. He readily admitted he couldn't draw worth beans, and I had to agree with him. I have always been mildly amused by Ben's biographies and chided him that I was never included, and that certain periods of his life were always glossed over with vague references to odd jobs such as milkman and playing in an army band. You could never stay mad at Ben for any length of time. He charmed his way into many successful careers and earned every award given him. We were actually neighbours in the Toronto Don Valley Woods Townhouse development in the 1960s when I was beginning my writing career and we lost touch later, but I followed his career with interest. Ben Wicks was the classic example of a penniless immigrant making good. He did it with a mixture of some talent and a lot of hard work and natural charm. John Fisher, 2010{{Citation needed|reason=long quotation needs reference|date=September 2018}}}}

Canada Games published a board game in collaboration with Ben called "Quick Picks With Ben Wicks" which is similar in game play to [[Pictionary]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://boardgamegeek.com/boardgame/14375/quick-pics | access-date=15 June 2017| title=Quick Pics}}</ref>

He also opened a [[pub]] in Toronto's [[Cabbagetown (Toronto)|Cabbagetown]] district named ''The Ben Wicks''.<ref>[http://www.benwickspub.com The Ben Wicks, 424 Parliament St., Toronto, ON  M5A 3A2]</ref> The [[Parliament Street (Toronto)|Parliament Street]] pub was sold to new owners in July 2013. However, a [[blue plaque]] commemorating Wicks has been installed on the railing and a wall-sized outdoor cartoon by Wicks has been retained.

Wicks was also known for his humanitarian work. He used his illustrations to publicize the plight of civilian sufferers of the [[Biafran War]] in [[Nigeria]], and became a supporter of [[Oxfam]]. During the [[1984–1985 famine in Ethiopia]], he organized Cartoonists for Africa, raising money and awareness. Wicks spent much time in his later decades promoting literacy among children.

In 1986, he was made a Member of the [[Order of Canada]].<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.gg.ca/honour.aspx?id=1762&t=12&ln=Wicks|title= Ben Wicks, C.M.|author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.--> |website= Governor General of Canada|access-date= 21 October 2016}}</ref>

In 1997, he donated material to the [[Toronto Metropolitan University|Ryerson University]] archives.

Wicks died of [[cancer]] in 2000 at age 73.<ref>{{cite news |last= Hermant|first= Norman|date= 10 September 2000|title= The pen and Ben Wicks|url= http://www.cbc.ca/archives/entry/the-pen-and-ben-wicks|publisher= CBC Digital Archives|access-date= 21 October 2016}}</ref>

In Wicks' memory, the annual  "Ben Wicks Award" was given from 2001 to 2010 to young talented cartoonists by [[Regional Maple Leaf Communications]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://regionalmapleleaf.com/news/ben-wicks-award |title=Ben Wicks Award | Regional Maple Leaf Corp |publisher=Regionalmapleleaf.com |access-date=2019-08-17 |archive-date=2020-05-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200513114519/https://regionalmapleleaf.com/news/ben-wicks-award |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://regionalmapleleaf.com/news/ben-wicks |title=Ben Wicks | Regional Maple Leaf Corp |publisher=Regionalmapleleaf.com |date=2000-09-10 |access-date=2019-08-17}}</ref>

In May 2007, Wicks was the subject of a court case, as his children tried to reclaim 2,408 vintage drawings left behind in a 1992 move.<ref>{{cite news |author=John Goddard |title=Who owns Wicks cartoons? |url=https://www.thestar.com/news/2007/05/15/who_owns_wicks_cartoons.html |work=The Toronto Star |date=May 15, 2007}}</ref> The family won the case and the court ordered the drawings to be returned to them.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/2007/05/31/family_of_ben_wicks_wins_back_misplaced_drawings.html|title=Family of Ben Wicks wins back misplaced drawings|newspaper=The Toronto Star|date=31 May 2007}}</ref>

==Works==
* ''Ben Wicks' Canada'', McClelland and Stewart Limited, Toronto, 1976, {{ISBN|0-7710-8983-X}}
* ''Waiting for the All Clear'', Bloomsbury, London, 1990, {{ISBN|0-7475-0667-1}}
* ''No Time to Wave Goodbye'', Stoddart, Toronto, 1988, {{ISBN|0-7737-2215-7}}
* ''[[Katie and Orbie]] Save the Planet'', 1991 (as illustrator), {{ISBN|1-5501-3322-5}}, {{ISBN|1-5501-3324-1}}, {{ISBN|1-5501-3326-8}}, {{ISBN|1-5501-3328-4}}
* The Day They Took the Children, Stoddart, Toronto,1989, {{ISBN|0-7737-2333-1}}
* Dawn of the Promised Land, The Creation of Israel, Stoddart,1997, {{ISBN|0-7737-3050-8}}
